text.title: filesystem-as-working-memory Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for FS-as-working-memory: offload thresholds, tool surface, taxonomy, persistence, compaction ordering.

rule.statement: Any tool whose raw output exceeds ~2000 tokens MUST write the full output to the agent's FS and return only a path plus a short snippet (first ~200 tokens or a structured digest).
rule.rationale: Offload is lossless; the agent can re-read at any precision. Holding raw outputs inline squeezes references out of context.

rule.statement: Expose exactly four FS tools to the agent: `write_file(path, content)`, `read_file(path, offset?, limit?)`, `ls(path?)`, `grep(pattern, path?)`; do NOT add separate copy/move/append tools.
rule.rationale: Four primitives compose like Unix utilities. Adding move/copy/append bloats the tool list and makes the agent pick wrong tools.

rule.statement: The agent SKILL or system prompt MUST declare a stable top-level taxonomy (e.g. `search/`, `docs/`, `plan/`, `scratch/`); the agent MUST NOT invent ad-hoc top-level folders per call.
rule.rationale: A predictable taxonomy makes `ls` a useful navigation aid; ad-hoc paths force the agent to remember names it cannot discover.

rule.statement: The FS backing store MUST commit atomically with the conversation checkpoint (same store/transaction, or paired with a write-ahead reconciliation step); resume MUST not produce dangling paths.
rule.rationale: When FS and conversation persist independently, restart leaves the agent with paths that 404 or summaries describing files that no longer exist.

rule.statement: LLM-based compaction MUST fire only when (a) context utilisation > 85% AND (b) no offloadable blob > 2K tokens remains inline; offload must always be tried first.
rule.rationale: Offload is lossless and reversible; compaction is lossy and one-way. Reversing the order burns information that could have been kept.
